title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Emotional Abuse
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy helps survivors of emotional abuse heal by rewiring the nervous system and reclaiming bodily safety.
body:
Understanding Emotional Abuse and Its Impact on the Body

Emotional abuse often leaves invisible wounds that conventional talk therapy alone struggles to address. Unlike physical abuse, emotional abuse assaults the sense of self through chronic invalidation, manipulation, verbal attacks, and neglect. These experiences create a deep imprint not only in the mind but also in the body’s nervous system. As Dr. Bessel van der Kolk emphasizes in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma is stored somatically; the body remembers what the mind may try to forget.

Survivors often report feeling stuck in cycles of anxiety, shame, hypervigilance, or numbness. These reactions are not psychological failures but adaptive nervous system responses shaped by prolonged threats to safety. Emotional abuse teaches the body that danger is constant, triggering fight, flight, freeze, or collapse responses long after the abusive situation ends.

The Limits of Talk Therapy for Emotional Abuse Survivors

While cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) and other traditional modalities provide valuable insights, many survivors find that talking alone doesn't dissipate the persistent somatic symptoms. Dr. Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic psychology, notes that trauma disrupts the natural flow of sensory and motor information in the nervous system, which talk therapy cannot directly access.

Survivors may experience symptoms such as chronic tension, dissociation, or unexplained pain that resist verbal explanation. This disconnect between mind and body often leads to a sense of alienation from one’s own experience, complicating recovery efforts.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is a body-based approach to trauma healing that focuses on the felt sense—the internal bodily experience—and the regulation of the nervous system. It draws from the work of Peter Levine (Somatic Experiencing), Stephen Porges (Polyvagal Theory), and Deb Dana, who emphasize the nervous system’s role in trauma recovery.

Instead of rehashing traumatic memories verbally, somatic therapy helps survivors track and renegotiate bodily sensations associated with past emotional abuse. This process enables the nervous system to complete incomplete defensive responses and restore a sense of safety and agency.

How Somatic Therapy Works for Emotional Abuse Survivors

Emotional abuse creates subtle but chronic dysregulation of the autonomic nervous system (ANS), often manifesting as a persistent sense of threat without clear external danger. Somatic therapy helps survivors identify these patterns and develop new nervous system responses through:

  • Tracking bodily sensations: Learning to notice tightness, heat, tension, or numbness linked to distress.
  • Resourcing and grounding: Cultivating internal and external anchors for safety, such as breath, posture, or safe relationships.
  • Pendulation: Moving between states of activation and calm to build nervous system flexibility.
  • Completing defensive responses: Allowing the body to express the fight, flight, or freeze impulses that were blocked during abuse.

These interventions recalibrate the nervous system, reducing hypervigilance and dissociation while increasing the capacity for self-regulation and presence.

Scientific Foundations: The Polyvagal Perspective

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a neurobiological framework explaining how emotional abuse impairs safety cues in the autonomic nervous system. The theory describes three neural circuits that regulate our responses to safety and threat:

  • The ventral vagal complex: Supports social engagement and calm states.
  • The sympathetic nervous system: Mobilizes fight or flight responses.
  • The dorsal vagal complex: Triggers shutdown or freeze responses.

Emotional abuse often conditions the nervous system to default to sympathetic or dorsal vagal states even in safe contexts, leading to chronic stress and dissociation. Somatic therapy’s focus on nervous system regulation helps reactivate the ventral vagal pathway, restoring social connection and emotional resilience.

Case Illustration: Healing Through Somatic Awareness

Consider the case of "Sarah," a survivor of emotional abuse who struggled with persistent anxiety and numbness despite years of talk therapy. Through somatic therapy, Sarah learned to notice the tightness in her chest and the shallow breathing that accompanied feelings of shame. By grounding in her breath and gently allowing the body’s urge to shake and release tension, she gradually reclaimed a sense of safety in her body.

Her nervous system recalibrated over months, reducing panic attacks and dissociative episodes. Sarah’s experience reflects what Francine Shapiro observed in EMDR therapy: engaging the body alongside the mind mobilizes healing pathways inaccessible through words alone.

Integrating Somatic Therapy Into Your Healing Journey

For survivors of emotional abuse, incorporating somatic therapy can complement existing treatment or serve as a primary healing modality. Here are practical steps to begin:

  • Find a qualified somatic therapist: Look for practitioners trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy (Pat Ogden), or Polyvagal-informed therapy.
  • Practice body awareness: Daily check-ins to notice sensations without judgment improve mind-body connection.
  • Use grounding techniques: Simple practices like deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, or feeling feet on the floor help regulate the nervous system.
  • Be patient with your nervous system: Healing is non-linear; small shifts accumulate over time.

The Role of Self-Compassion and Internal Family Systems

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model complements somatic work by addressing internal parts shaped by emotional abuse—such as the critical inner voice or the vulnerable child part. IFS encourages compassionate witnessing and unburdening of these parts.

Combined with somatic regulation, IFS helps survivors soften internal conflict and build a cohesive self, a crucial step toward lasting recovery. Gabor Maté also highlights the importance of self-compassion in healing trauma, noting it re-establishes safety internally when external safety was lacking.

“Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you.” – Gabor Maté

Conclusion: Reclaiming Safety in the Body

Emotional abuse leaves deep marks on the nervous system that require more than words to heal. Somatic therapy offers a science-based, body-centered path to recovery by helping survivors rewire their physiological responses and reclaim safety within themselves.

By tuning into bodily sensations, completing stuck defensive responses, and nurturing nervous system regulation, survivors can move from survival to thriving. The journey takes courage and patience, but with somatic tools and compassionate support, healing is possible.

If you’ve tried talk therapy but still feel trapped in old patterns, consider integrating somatic therapy into your healing toolkit. Your body holds the key to your recovery.

References:

  • van der Kolk, B. (2015). The Body Keeps the Score. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Schwartz, R. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts. Random House Canada.
  • Shapiro, F. (2017). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy for emotional abuse?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body’s sensations and nervous system regulation rather than only verbal processing, helping to resolve trauma stored physically.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help with symptoms like anxiety and dissociation?
faqs.a: Yes, by recalibrating the nervous system, somatic therapy reduces anxiety and dissociation commonly seen in survivors of emotional abuse.

faqs.q: Is somatic therapy safe to do without a therapist?
faqs.a: While some body awareness practices can be done independently, working with a trained somatic therapist is recommended for safely navigating trauma.

faqs.q: How long does somatic therapy take to show results?
faqs.a: Healing timelines vary; some notice improvements within weeks, but nervous system regulation and trauma resolution often take months of consistent work.
